steps.title: Identify the main clause structure
steps.content: The sentence follows the pattern “the reason that [complex noun clause] is that [explanation].” The complex noun clause is “the rate of heart disease seems lower... while similarly ___” — it contains both an independent clause (the rate is lower) and a dependent clause beginning with “while.” All of this forms a single subject noun phrase that must reach the main verb “is” uninterrupted.

steps.title: Apply the subject-verb boundary rule
steps.content:
In Standard English, no punctuation may separate a subject from its main verb, regardless of how complex the subject is.

The dependent clause “while similarly stressed” remains part of the subject; it doesn't require punctuation before “is.” The remaining choices incorrectly insert a comma, semicolon, or colon between the subject and verb.

That confirms A is correct.
